text: When I was 27 years old, I left a very demanding job in management consulting for a job that was even more demanding, teaching.
question: What did the speaker do when she was 27 years old?

answers.answer: She left her job in consulting to become a teacher.
She started a business in management.
She decided to study math at university.

text: I went to teach seventh graders math in the New York City public schools.
question: Who did the speaker teach?

answers.answer: Seventh grade students in New York City.

text: And like any teacher, I made quizzes and tests, I gave out homework assignments.
question: What tasks did the speaker do as a teacher?

answers.answer: She made quizzes and homework for students.
She cleaned the classroom every day.

text: When the work came back, I calculated grades.
question: What did the speaker do after collecting students' work?

answers.answer: She returned the work without checking.

text: What struck me was that IQ was not the only difference between my best and my worst students.
question: What did the speaker notice about her students?

answers.answer: IQ was not the only factor in students' performance.
IQ was the most important factor for success.
All students had the same IQ.
IQ scores were very high for everyone.

text: Some of my strongest performers did not have stratospheric IQ scores.
question: Did all strong students have high IQ scores?

answers.answer: No, some strong students had average IQ scores.
Yes, all strong students had very high IQ scores.
The speaker did not test IQ.
Only weak students had high IQ scores.

text: Some of my smartest kids weren't doing so well.
question: Did all smart students do well in school?

answers.answer: Yes, all smart students did very well.
Smart students did not take tests.
The smartest students left the school.

text: The kinds of things you need to learn in seventh grade math, sure, they're hard.
question: How does the speaker describe seventh grade math?

answers.answer: It is hard, but possible to learn.
It is very easy for everyone.
It is not taught in school.

text: But these concepts are not impossible.
question: Are seventh grade math concepts impossible?

text: And I was firmly convinced that every one of my students could learn the material if they worked hard and long enough.
question: What does the speaker believe about her students?

answers.answer: All students can learn with enough effort.
Only the smartest students can learn.
Most students cannot learn this material.

text: I came to the conclusion that what we need in education is a much better understanding of students and learning from a motivational perspective, from a psychological perspective.
question: What does the speaker think is needed in education?

answers.answer: A better understanding of students and learning psychology.

text: In education, the one thing we know how to measure best is IQ.
question: What is easily measured in education?

text: But what if doing well in school and in life depends on much more than your ability to learn quickly and easily?
question: What does the speaker suggest about success?

answers.answer: Success depends on more than learning ability.
IQ is the only thing that matters.

text: So I left the classroom and I went to graduate school to become a psychologist.
question: What did the speaker do after teaching?

answers.answer: She went to graduate school to study psychology.
She returned to high school as a student.
She started a consulting job again.

text: I started studying kids and adults in all kinds of super challenging settings.
question: What did the speaker study?

answers.answer: Children and adults in difficult settings.

text: And in every study, my question was, who is successful here and why?
question: What question did the speaker ask in her studies?

text: My research team and I went to West Point Military Academy.
question: Where did the speaker and her team go?

text: We tried to predict which cadets would stay in military training and which would drop out.
question: What did the speaker try to predict at West Point?

answers.answer: Which cadets would continue or leave training.

text: We went to the National Spelling Bee and tried to predict which children would advance farthest in competition.
question: What did the speaker study at the Spelling Bee?

text: We studied rookie teachers working in really tough neighborhoods, asking which teachers are still going to be here in teaching by the end of the school year.
question: What did the speaker study about rookie teachers?

answers.answer: Which teachers would stay until the end of the school year.
Which teachers would move to another city.
Which teachers had the highest IQ.

text: And of those, who will be the most effective at improving learning outcomes for their students.
question: What was measured about the teachers?

text: We partnered with private companies, asking which of these salespeople is going to keep their jobs, and who's going to earn the most money.
question: What did the speaker study in private companies?

answers.answer: Which salespeople would succeed and earn more money.
Which salespeople would take vacation first.
Which salespeople had the highest IQ.
Which salespeople liked math the most.

text: In all those very different contexts, one characteristic emerged as a significant predictor of success.
question: What did the studies find?

answers.answer: One trait predicted success across different areas.
IQ was always the best predictor.

text: And it wasn't social intelligence, it wasn't good looks, physical health, and it wasn't IQ.
question: Which traits were NOT predictors of success?

answers.answer: Social intelligence, looks, health, and IQ.

text: It was grit.
question: What characteristic predicted success?

text: Grit is passion and perseverance for very long-term goals.
question: How does the speaker define grit?

answers.answer: Passion and perseverance for long-term goals.

text: Grit is having stamina.
question: Another way to describe grit is:

text: Grit is sticking with your future, day in, day out, not just for the week, not just for the month, but for years, and working really hard to make that future a reality.
question: What does grit involve?

answers.answer: Working hard every day for long-term goals.
Finishing tasks only in the short term.

text: Grit is living life like it's a marathon, not a sprint.
question: How does the speaker compare grit?

answers.answer: Life with grit is like a marathon, not a sprint.
Life with grit is very fast like a sprint.
Grit is like winning a race quickly.
Grit is not important in life.

text: A few years ago, I started studying grit in the Chicago Public Schools.
question: Where did the speaker study grit?

text: I asked thousands of high school juniors to take grit questionnaires and then waited around more than a year to see who would graduate.
question: What did the speaker do with high school juniors?

answers.answer: Gave grit questionnaires and checked graduation after a year.
Asked them about IQ scores only.
Gave them math tests every month.

text: Turns out that grittier kids were significantly more likely to graduate, even when I matched them on every characteristic I could measure.
question: Who was more likely to graduate?

text: Things like family income, standardized achievement test scores, even how safe kids felt when they were at school.
question: Which factors did NOT fully explain graduation?

answers.answer: Income, test scores, and safety feelings.

text: So it's not just at West Point or the National Spelling Bee that grit matters, it's also in school, especially for kids at risk for dropping out.
question: Where does grit matter?

answers.answer: In school and competitions like West Point and Spelling Bee.

text: To me, the most shocking thing about grit is how little we know, how little science knows about building it.
question: What surprised the speaker about grit?

answers.answer: Science knows very little about building it.
Everyone knows exactly how to build grit.

text: Every day, parents and teachers ask me, how do I build grit in kids?
question: Who asks the speaker questions about grit?

text: What do I do to teach kids a solid work ethic?
question: What does the speaker wonder about teaching?

answers.answer: How to teach a strong work ethic.

text: How do I keep them motivated for the long run?
question: What is the speaker asking about motivation?

answers.answer: How to keep kids motivated long-term.

text: The honest answer is, I don't know.
question: Does the speaker know how to build grit?

text: What I do know is that talent doesn't make you gritty.
question: Does talent create grit?

answers.answer: No, talent alone does not create grit.
Yes, talent is enough for grit.

text: Our data show very clearly that there are many talented individuals who simply do not follow through on their commitments.
question: What does the data show about talented individuals?

answers.answer: They may fail to follow through on commitments.

text: In fact, in our data, grit is usually unrelated or even inversely related to measures of talent.
question: How is grit related to talent?

answers.answer: Grit is not directly related to talent.
Grit and talent are the same.

text: So far, the best idea I've heard about building grit in kids is something called growth mindset.
question: What helps build grit according to the speaker?

text: This is an idea developed at Stanford University by Carol Dweck, and it is the belief that the ability to learn is not fixed, that it can change with your effort.
question: What does growth mindset teach?

answers.answer: Ability to learn can improve with effort.
IQ is fixed and cannot change.

text: Dr. Dweck has shown that when kids read and learn about the brain and how it changes and grows in response to challenge, they're much more likely to persevere when they fail because they don't believe that failure is a permanent condition.
question: Why do kids with growth mindset persevere?

answers.answer: They believe failure is not permanent.
